text: 49 ADVANCED SECONDARY CURRICULUM TOPIC 16: Error Analysis Duration: 16 Periods Competency: The learner analyses errors in mathematical operations and functions to understand the role of precision and accuracy in problem-solving. Learning Outcomes The learner should be able to: Suggested Learning Activities Suggested Assessment Strategies a) examine the propagation of errors in mathematical operations and functions. (k, u, v/a, s, gs) a) In pairs, through experimentation and discussions, learners: i) measure lengths, weights, volumes, thicknesses, temperatures, timings etc. using various measurement tools of varying levels of precision and use the measurements to compute and compare sums, differences, products and ratios of similar measurements but from different tools, ii) describe errors, sources of errors, the terms used in error analysis and note cases where errors may arise in mathematical computations and real life. b) In groups, learners through peer-teaching explore how to derive and compute absolute, relative and percentage errors, minimum and maximum values/ limits of accuracy in expressions and make
subject: mathematics

text: to derive and compute absolute, relative and percentage errors, minimum and maximum values/ limits of accuracy in expressions and make presentations. c) Through guided discovery, learners use their knowledge of differentiation to derive the formulae for error propagation in functions. a) Observe learners as they discuss propagation of errors in mathematical operations and functions noting their ability to: i) work hard for self- reliance, ii) interact effectively with others, iii) work effectively in diverse teams. b) Converse with learners and prompt them to explain how errors arise in measurement, determine errors in different mathematical operations, determine the minimum and maximum values/ limits of accuracy, and how errors propagate in functions, focusing on learners’ ability to adapt and manipulate the knowledge of errors and its relevance in real life. c) Assess learners’ written work or ICT aided oral presentations to evaluate the accuracy in describing error terms, types and sources of errors and the appropriate formula for propagation of errors in

text: 51 ADVANCED SECONDARY CURRICULUM Senior Six Term 1 TOPIC 17: Vectors Duration: 22 Periods Competency: The learner applies vector concepts to model and solve mathematical and physical problems in 2D and 3D spaces. text: 60 PRINCIPAL MATHEMATICS SYLLABUS TOPIC 21: Trapezium Rule Duration: 06 Periods Competency: The learner applies the trapezium rule to estimate definite integrals and areas under a curve to solve problems involving numerical integration. Learning Outcomes The learner should be able to: Suggested Learning Activities Suggested Assessment Strategies a) evaluate definite integrals and areas under curves using the trapezium rule. (k, u, v/a, s, gs) a) Through discussion, learners: i) sketch smooth curves on a piece of paper dividing it into trapeziums with equal step widths, ii) approximate the area under the curve by calculating the sum of the trapeziums’ areas, iii) calculate the sum of the areas of the trapeziums of your shape for varying step widths, iv) observe a general pattern to derive the formula for the trapezium rule and use the rule to estimate definite integrals and areas under curves. a) Observe learners as they sketch curves and evaluate areas over certain intervals. Focus on learners’ ability to:
subject: mathematics

Focus on learners’ ability to pay attention to areas that fall under the x-axis and how learners use them to explain the difference between an integral and the area under a curve. c) Assess learners’ presentations and written work on areas under a curve using trapezium rule with different step sizes. Examine their ability to explain the concepts clearly.

text: 68 PRINCIPAL MATHEMATICS SYLLABUS b) analyse locus of a circle to solve contextual problems in geometry, motion, engineering, and architecture. (k, u, v/a, s, gs) a) In pairs, learners use a compass, pencil and paper or any other material to review the centre, radius of a circle with reference to coordinate geometry 1 to determine the equation of a circle b) In pairs, learners use a compass, pencil and paper to determine the tangent to a circle, its relationship to the radius and length of tangent drawn from a point outside the circle. c) In groups, learners use concepts of perpendicular lines or differentiation to determine the tangent to a circle at a point. d) Learners use a compass, pencil and paper to deduce the conditions for external, internal and orthogonal intersection of circles. a) Observe learners as they determine the characteristics and properties of a circle. text: 78 PRINCIPAL MATHEMATICS SYLLABUS 3.0 ASSESSMENT 3.1 Assessing Mathematics The adapted curriculum sets new expectations for learning, with a shift from Objectives to Learning Outcomes that focus mainly on application of knowledge and deeper learning that leads to acquisition of skills. These Learning Outcomes require a different approach to assessment. The Learning Outcomes in the syllabuses are set out in terms of Knowledge, Understanding, Skills, Values, and Attitudes. This is what is referred to by the letters k, u, s, v & a. It is not possible to assess attitudes in the same way as knowledge, understanding and skills because they are more personal and variable and are long-term aspirations. This does not mean that values and attitudes are not important or cannot be assessed. They too can be assessed but not easily done through tests and examination. Values and attitudes can be assessed over a period of time through observing and having interactions with the learner. So, this guidance
subject: mathematics

text: attitudes can be assessed over a period of time through observing and having interactions with the learner. So, this guidance section focuses on knowledge, skills and understanding. Each has its own implications for learning and assessment. To assess knowledge and its application, understanding and skills, we need to look for different things. Knowledge can be assessed to some extent through written tests, but the assessment of skills, application of what is learnt and deeper understanding requires different approaches. Because of this, the role of the teacher in assessment becomes much more important. 3.2 Formative Assessment In this aligned curriculum, the teacher’s assessment role is not to write tests for learners, but to make professional judgement about learners’ learning in the course of the normal teaching and learning process. The professional judgement is about how far the learner achieves the Learning Outcomes that are set out in this syllabus. To make this judgement the teacher needs to look at how well
subject: mathematics

text: Outcomes that are set out in this syllabus. To make this judgement the teacher needs to look at how well the learners are performing in terms of each Learning Outcome. The formative assessment opportunities occur in three forms. They can be done through: a) Observation– watching learners working (good for assessing skills, values and attitudes) b) Conversation – asking questions and talking to learners (good for assessing knowledge and understanding) c) Product – appraising the learner’s work (writing, report, translation, calculation, presentation, map, diagram, model, drawing, painting, etc.). In this context, a “product” is seen as something physical and permanent that the teacher can keep and look at, not something that the learner says. When all three are used, the information from any one can be checked against the other two forms of assessment opportunity (e.g. evidence from observation can be checked against evidence from conversation and product). This is often referred to as triangulation 3.0 ASSESSMENT

text: 79 ADVANCED SECONDARY CURRICULUM 3.3 Assessing Generic Skills The Generic Skills have been built into the syllabuses and are part of the Learning Outcomes. It is therefore not necessary to assess them separately. It is the increasingly complex context of the subject content that provides progression in the Generic Skills, and so they are assessed as part of the subject Learning Outcomes. Assessing generic skills is done with the help of an observation checklist and scoring rubric. 3.4 Assessing Values/Attitudes It is not possible to assess values and attitudes in the same way as knowledge, understanding and skills because they are more personal and variable and are long-term aspirations. This does not mean that attitudes are not important. It means that we must value things that we cannot easily assess through tests and examination. However, values and attitudes can be assessed over a long period of time through observing and interactions. 3.5 Assessment of Project-based Learning Project-based learning is a
subject: mathematics

text: assessed over a long period of time through observing and interactions. 3.5 Assessment of Project-based Learning Project-based learning is a teaching method in which learners or participants gain knowledge and skills by engaging for an extended period of time to investigate and respond to an authentic challenge. text: 80 PRINCIPAL MATHEMATICS SYLLABUS 3.6 Examinations There will be only one school based summative assessment at the end of the year. There will no longer be examinations or tests set at the beginning and end of every term. Instead, there will be a summing up of on-going teacher assessments made in the context of learning through end of topic scenario-based tasks (Activities of Integration). The learners will also be subjected to the end of cycle assessment for certification. 3.7 Record keeping In competency-based learning, accurate and comprehensive record keeping is crucial to track learners' progress and achievements. Therefore, the teacher and school must keep accurate records about learners’ achievement. Various assessment tools and strategies are employed to capture learners' demonstration of abilities and achievements, including observation checklists, rubrics, and scoring grids. These tools provide a holistic picture of learners' strengths, weaknesses, and areas for improvement. The collected data and evidence from these assessments are correctly recorded and maintained in learners'

text: 81 ADVANCED SECONDARY CURRICULUM GLOSSARY OF KEY TERMS Term Definition competency-based curriculum One in which learners develop the ability to apply their learning with confidence in a range of situations. differentiation The design or adaptation of learning experiences to suit an individual learner’s needs, strengths, preferences, and abilities. formative assessment The process of judging the learner’s performance, by interpreting the responses to tasks, in order to gauge progress and inform subsequent learning steps. generic skills Skills which are deployed in all subjects, and which enhance the learning of those subjects. These skills also equip young people for work and for life. inclusion An approach to planning learning experiences which allows each student to feel confident, respected and safe and equipped to learn at his or her full potential. learning outcome A statement which specifies what the learner should know, under-stand, or be able to do within a particular aspect of a subject. process skill A capability acquired by following the

PRINCIPAL MATHEMATICS SYLLABUS
National Curriculum Development Centre

CONTACT US:
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Cell Biology > Cell Structure and Function
Subtopic: Eukaryotic Cell Organelles
Content: Nucleus: contains DNA, controls cell activities, bounded by nuclear envelope with pores. Mitochondria: aerobic respiration, double membrane, cristae increase surface area, own DNA. Rough ER: studded with ribosomes, protein synthesis and transport. Smooth ER: lipid synthesis, detoxification. Golgi apparatus: modifies, packages, secretes proteins in vesicles. Ribosomes: 80S (60S+40S), site of translation. Lysosomes: hydrolytic enzymes, intracellular digestion. Cell membrane: phospholipid bilayer, fluid mosaic model (Singer-Nicolson), integral and peripheral proteins, cholesterol for fluidity.
Competences: Draw and label eukaryotic cell with organelles, Explain function of each organelle, Compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, Describe fluid mosaic model
subject: biology
topic: Cell Biology > Cell Structure and Function

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Cell Biology > Cell Structure and Function
Subtopic: Membrane Transport
Content: Diffusion: passive movement from high to low concentration. Facilitated diffusion: via channel or carrier proteins (passive). Osmosis: movement of water through selectively permeable membrane from high water potential to low water potential. Water potential: ψ = ψs + ψp (solute potential + pressure potential). Active transport: against concentration gradient, requires ATP (e.g., sodium-potassium pump: 3Na⁺ out, 2K⁺ in). Endocytosis: phagocytosis (solids) and pinocytosis (liquids). Exocytosis: secretion of substances in vesicles.
Competences: Explain diffusion, osmosis, and active transport, Calculate water potential, Describe the sodium-potassium pump, Design osmosis experiments
subject: biology
topic: Cell Biology > Cell Structure and Function

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Cell Biology > Cell Division
Subtopic: Mitosis
Content: Mitosis produces two genetically identical daughter cells. Prophase: chromatin condenses, nuclear envelope breaks down, spindle forms. Metaphase: chromosomes align at metaphase plate. Anaphase: centromeres split, sister chromatids pulled to poles. Telophase: nuclear envelopes reform, chromosomes decondense. Cytokinesis: cytoplasm divides. Significance: growth, repair, asexual reproduction. Mitotic index = dividing cells / total cells. Uncontrolled mitosis → cancer (tumour suppressor genes p53 and Rb, oncogenes).
Competences: Describe each phase of mitosis, Calculate mitotic index, Relate uncontrolled division to cancer
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Cell Biology > Cell Division
Subtopic: Meiosis
Content: Meiosis produces four genetically different haploid cells. Meiosis I: homologous pairs separate (reduction division). Prophase I: crossing over at chiasmata. Metaphase I: independent assortment of bivalents. Meiosis II: sister chromatids separate (like mitosis). Sources of genetic variation: crossing over, independent assortment, random fertilisation. Non-disjunction: failure to separate → aneuploidy (e.g., Down syndrome = trisomy 21). Significance: gamete production, maintains chromosome number, generates variation.
Competences: Compare mitosis and meiosis, Explain how crossing over and independent assortment generate variation, Describe non-disjunction and its consequences
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Cell Biology > Enzymes
Subtopic: Enzyme Kinetics and Inhibition
Content: Enzymes are biological catalysts (proteins). Lock-and-key model: substrate fits exactly into active site. Induced-fit model: active site changes shape to fit substrate (more accurate). Factors affecting rate: temperature (optimum ~37°C for human enzymes), pH (optimum varies), substrate concentration, enzyme concentration. Michaelis-Menten kinetics: V = Vmax[S]/(Km + [S]). Km = substrate concentration at ½Vmax. Competitive inhibition: inhibitor competes for active site, increases apparent Km, Vmax unchanged. Non-competitive inhibition: inhibitor binds elsewhere, changes shape of active site, Vmax decreases, Km unchanged.
Competences: Explain lock-and-key and induced-fit models, Interpret Michaelis-Menten curves, Distinguish competitive from non-competitive inhibition
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Molecular Biology and Genetics > DNA and Protein Synthesis
Subtopic: DNA Replication
Content: Semi-conservative replication (Meselson-Stahl experiment). Steps: helicase unwinds double helix, SSB proteins stabilise single strands, primase adds RNA primer, DNA polymerase III adds nucleotides 5'→3', leading strand synthesised continuously, lagging strand in Okazaki fragments, DNA ligase joins fragments, DNA polymerase I replaces primers with DNA. Accuracy: proofreading by DNA polymerase, mismatch repair enzymes.
Competences: Describe semi-conservative replication, Explain roles of helicase, polymerase, ligase, Describe leading and lagging strand synthesis
subject: biology
topic: Molecular Biology and Genetics > DNA and Protein Synthesis

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Molecular Biology and Genetics > DNA and Protein Synthesis
Subtopic: Transcription and Translation
Content: Transcription: RNA polymerase binds to promoter, unwinds DNA, synthesises mRNA 5'→3' using template strand (3'→5'). In eukaryotes: pre-mRNA → splicing (introns removed, exons joined) → mature mRNA. mRNA leaves nucleus via nuclear pores. Translation: ribosome binds to mRNA at start codon (AUG). tRNA with complementary anticodon brings amino acid. Peptide bond forms between amino acids. Ribosome moves along mRNA. Stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA) terminate translation. Genetic code: triplet, degenerate, universal, non-overlapping.
Competences: Describe transcription in prokaryotes and eukaryotes, Explain translation step by step, Use codon table to determine amino acid sequences
subject: biology
topic: Molecular Biology and Genetics > DNA and Protein Synthesis

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Molecular Biology and Genetics > Inheritance
Subtopic: Mendelian Genetics and Extensions
Content: Mendel's First Law (Segregation): two alleles separate during gamete formation. Second Law (Independent Assortment): genes on different chromosomes inherited independently. Monohybrid cross: 3:1 ratio in F2. Dihybrid cross: 9:3:3:1 ratio in F2. Test cross: cross with homozygous recessive. Codominance: both alleles expressed (e.g., blood groups ABO: IA, IB, i). Incomplete dominance: blended phenotype. Sex-linked inheritance: genes on X chromosome (e.g., haemophilia, colour blindness) — more common in males. Autosomal linkage: genes on same chromosome, do not assort independently.
Competences: Solve monohybrid, dihybrid, and sex-linked problems, Draw Punnett squares and genetic diagrams, Explain codominance and incomplete dominance
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Molecular Biology and Genetics > Inheritance
Subtopic: Mutations and Genetic Engineering
Content: Gene mutations: point mutations — substitution (silent, missense, nonsense), insertion, deletion (frameshift). Chromosome mutations: deletion, duplication, inversion, translocation, aneuploidy, polyploidy. Causes: spontaneous errors, mutagens (UV, chemicals, radiation). Genetic engineering: restriction enzymes cut DNA at specific sequences, ligase joins fragments, vectors (plasmids) carry genes into host cells. PCR: amplifies DNA (denature 95°C, anneal 55°C, extend 72°C). Applications: insulin production, gene therapy, GM crops.
Competences: Describe types of gene and chromosome mutations, Explain genetic engineering techniques, Discuss applications and ethical issues of GMOs
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Physiology > Nervous System
Subtopic: Nerve Impulse and Synaptic Transmission
Content: Resting potential: -70mV (K⁺ leak channels, Na⁺/K⁺ pump). Action potential: depolarisation (Na⁺ channels open, Na⁺ rushes in → +40mV), repolarisation (K⁺ channels open, K⁺ rushes out), hyperpolarisation, refractory period. All-or-nothing principle. Saltatory conduction in myelinated neurons (faster). Synapse: action potential → Ca²⁺ influx → vesicles fuse → neurotransmitter (e.g., acetylcholine) released → binds receptors on postsynaptic membrane → new action potential. Neurotransmitter removed by enzymes (acetylcholinesterase).
Competences: Describe resting and action potentials, Explain synaptic transmission step by step, Compare myelinated and unmyelinated neurons
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Physiology > Endocrine System
Subtopic: Hormonal Control and Homeostasis
Content: Hormones: chemical messengers secreted by endocrine glands into blood. Insulin (β-cells of pancreas): lowers blood glucose → promotes glycogenesis, increases glucose uptake. Glucagon (α-cells): raises blood glucose → promotes glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis. Negative feedback: response reduces the stimulus. ADH (posterior pituitary): increases water reabsorption in collecting duct → concentrated urine. Adrenaline (adrenal medulla): fight-or-flight response. Thyroxine: metabolic rate regulation (iodine needed). Diabetes: Type 1 (no insulin), Type 2 (insulin resistance).
Competences: Explain insulin-glucagon regulation of blood glucose, Describe ADH and osmoregulation, Distinguish Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Physiology > Cardiovascular System
Subtopic: Heart Structure and Circulation
Content: Double circulation: pulmonary (heart → lungs → heart) and systemic (heart → body → heart). Heart chambers: right atrium receives deoxygenated blood via vena cava; right ventricle pumps to lungs via pulmonary artery; left atrium receives oxygenated blood via pulmonary veins; left ventricle pumps to body via aorta. Left ventricle wall thicker (higher pressure). Cardiac cycle: atrial systole → ventricular systole → diastole. Heart rate controlled by SAN (pacemaker), AVN, bundle of His, Purkinje fibres. Blood pressure: systolic/diastolic (120/80 mmHg normal).
Competences: Describe structure and function of the heart, Trace path of blood through double circulation, Explain the cardiac cycle and ECG
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Physiology > Respiration
Subtopic: Aerobic and Anaerobic Respiration
Content: Aerobic respiration: C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ → 6CO₂ + 6H₂O + 38 ATP. Stages: glycolysis (cytoplasm, glucose → 2 pyruvate, 2 ATP net), link reaction (pyruvate → acetyl CoA + CO₂), Krebs cycle (mitochondrial matrix, acetyl CoA → CO₂ + reduced NAD/FAD), oxidative phosphorylation (inner mitochondrial membrane, electron transport chain, chemiosmosis, 34 ATP). Anaerobic in animals: pyruvate → lactate (oxygen debt). Anaerobic in yeast: pyruvate → ethanol + CO₂ (fermentation). Respiratory quotient RQ = CO₂ produced / O₂ consumed. RQ = 1 for carbohydrates, <1 for fats, >1 for anaerobic.
Competences: Describe all stages of aerobic respiration, Compare aerobic and anaerobic respiration, Calculate and interpret respiratory quotient
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Ecology and Plant Biology > Ecosystems and Energy Flow
Subtopic: Food Chains, Nutrient Cycling, and Conservation
Content: Energy flow: sun → producers → primary consumers → secondary consumers → tertiary consumers. ~10% energy transfer efficiency between trophic levels (rest lost as heat via respiration). Pyramids of number, biomass, energy. Carbon cycle: photosynthesis removes CO₂, respiration/combustion release CO₂, decomposition recycles. Nitrogen cycle: nitrogen fixation (Rhizobium in root nodules, lightning), nitrification (NH₄⁺ → NO₂⁻ → NO₃⁻ by Nitrosomonas and Nitrobacter), denitrification (NO₃⁻ → N₂). Biodiversity: species richness × evenness. Conservation: in-situ (national parks), ex-situ (seed banks, zoos). Ugandan examples: Bwindi Impenetrable Forest (gorillas), Lake Victoria (Nile perch impact on cichlids).
Competences: Draw and explain food chains and webs, Describe carbon and nitrogen cycles, Discuss conservation strategies using Ugandan examples
subject: biology
topic: Ecology and Plant Biology > Ecosystems and Energy Flow
section: Food Chains, Nutrient Cycling, and Conservation

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Ecology and Plant Biology > Photosynthesis
Subtopic: Light and Dark Reactions
Content: Overall: 6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (light energy). Light-dependent reactions (thylakoid membranes): photolysis of water (2H₂O → 4H⁺ + 4e⁻ + O₂), electron transport chain, photophosphorylation (ATP), reduction of NADP⁺ → NADPH. Light-independent reactions (Calvin cycle, stroma): CO₂ fixation by RuBisCO (CO₂ + RuBP → 2GP), reduction of GP to G3P (using ATP + NADPH), regeneration of RuBP. Limiting factors: light intensity, CO₂ concentration, temperature. Law of limiting factors: rate determined by factor furthest from optimum.
Competences: Describe light-dependent and light-independent reactions, Explain role of RuBisCO in Calvin cycle, Interpret graphs of limiting factors
subject: biology

text:
Subject: Biology
Topic: Ecology and Plant Biology > Plant Transport
Subtopic: Transpiration and Translocation
Content: Transpiration: loss of water vapour from leaves through stomata. Transpiration stream: root hair cells → xylem → leaves. Cohesion-tension theory: water molecules cohere via hydrogen bonds, creating continuous column pulled up by transpiration. Factors affecting rate: temperature, humidity, wind speed, light intensity. Measured with potometer. Translocation: transport of sucrose and amino acids in phloem. Mass flow hypothesis (Münch): high pressure at source (leaves), low pressure at sink (roots, fruits). Loading: active transport of sucrose into sieve tubes (companion cells pump H⁺, sucrose co-transported).
Competences: Explain cohesion-tension theory, Describe factors affecting transpiration rate, Explain mass flow hypothesis for translocation
subject: biology
topic: Ecology and Plant Biology > Plant Transport

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Atomic Structure
Subtopic: Electronic Configuration and Orbitals
Content: Electrons occupy orbitals in order of increasing energy: 1s 2s 2p 3s 3p 4s 3d 4p... Aufbau principle: fill lowest energy orbitals first. Pauli exclusion: max 2 electrons per orbital with opposite spins. Hund's rule: electrons fill degenerate orbitals singly before pairing. Quantum numbers: n (principal), l (angular momentum, 0 to n-1), ml (-l to +l), ms (+½ or -½). s holds 2, p holds 6, d holds 10 electrons.
Competences: Write electronic configurations for elements 1-36, Apply Aufbau, Pauli, and Hund's rules, Relate electronic configuration to position in periodic table
subject: chemistry
topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Atomic Structure

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Chemical Bonding
Subtopic: Types of Chemical Bonds
Content: Ionic bonding: transfer of electrons between metals and non-metals, forming ions held by electrostatic attraction. Properties: high melting point, conduct when molten/dissolved, brittle. Covalent bonding: sharing of electron pairs between non-metals. Dative (coordinate) bond: both electrons from one atom. Metallic bonding: delocalised electrons in a sea around positive metal ions. Properties: malleable, conduct electricity, lustrous. Intermolecular forces: van der Waals (London dispersion), dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding (F, O, N with H).
Competences: Draw dot-and-cross diagrams for ionic and covalent compounds, Explain properties in terms of bonding, Identify and compare intermolecular forces
subject: chemistry
topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Chemical Bonding

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Chemical Bonding
Subtopic: Shapes of Molecules (VSEPR)
Content: VSEPR theory: electron pairs around central atom repel and adopt positions that minimise repulsion. Lone pair-lone pair > lone pair-bond pair > bond pair-bond pair repulsion. Linear (2 bonding pairs, 180°): CO₂, BeCl₂. Trigonal planar (3 bp, 120°): BF₃. Tetrahedral (4 bp, 109.5°): CH₄. Bent (2 bp + 2 lp, ~104.5°): H₂O. Trigonal pyramidal (3 bp + 1 lp, ~107°): NH₃. Octahedral (6 bp, 90°): SF₆.
Competences: Predict shapes of molecules using VSEPR, Draw 3D representations, Explain bond angle variations due to lone pairs
subject: chemistry
topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Chemical Bonding

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Atomic Structure and Bonding > Periodicity
Subtopic: Periodic Trends
Content: Atomic radius decreases across a period (increasing nuclear charge) and increases down a group (more shells). First ionisation energy generally increases across a period and decreases down a group. Electronegativity increases across period and decreases down group (Pauling scale: F = 4.0, most electronegative). Metallic character increases down a group and decreases across a period. Group trends: Group 2 (alkaline earth metals) reactivity increases down; Group 7 (halogens) reactivity decreases down.
Competences: Explain trends in atomic radius, ionisation energy, electronegativity, Compare Group 2 and Group 7 properties, Relate trends to electronic structure
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Organic Chemistry > Nomenclature and Isomerism
Subtopic: IUPAC Naming and Types of Isomerism
Content: IUPAC naming: identify longest carbon chain, number from end nearest substituent, name substituents alphabetically. Functional group suffixes: -ane (alkane), -ene (alkene), -ol (alcohol), -al (aldehyde), -one (ketone), -oic acid (carboxylic acid), -oate (ester). Structural isomerism: same molecular formula, different structural formula (chain, position, functional group). Stereoisomerism: E/Z (geometric) isomerism around C=C with different groups; optical isomerism with chiral carbon (4 different groups).
Competences: Name organic compounds using IUPAC rules, Identify all types of isomerism, Draw structural and displayed formulae
subject: chemistry
section: IUPAC Naming and Types of Isomerism

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Organic Chemistry > Reaction Mechanisms
Subtopic: Nucleophilic Substitution (SN1 and SN2)
Content: SN2: one-step concerted mechanism. Nucleophile attacks from backside (180°) as leaving group departs. Rate = k[substrate][nucleophile]. Favoured by: primary substrates, strong nucleophiles, polar aprotic solvents. Produces inversion of configuration (Walden inversion). SN1: two-step mechanism. Step 1: leaving group departs → carbocation (rate-determining). Step 2: nucleophile attacks. Rate = k[substrate]. Favoured by: tertiary substrates, weak nucleophiles, polar protic solvents. Produces racemic mixture.
Competences: Draw SN1 and SN2 mechanisms with curly arrows, Predict which mechanism is favoured, Predict stereochemical outcomes
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Organic Chemistry > Reaction Mechanisms
Subtopic: Elimination Reactions (E1 and E2)
Content: E2: one-step concerted. Strong base abstracts β-hydrogen, leaving group departs. Requires anti-periplanar geometry. Favoured by strong bases, primary/secondary substrates. E1: two-step via carbocation intermediate. Favoured by weak bases, tertiary substrates. Zaitsev's Rule: more substituted alkene is major product. Competition: SN2 vs E2 (strong base + primary = SN2; strong base + tertiary = E2). SN1 vs E1 (occur simultaneously with tertiary substrates).
Competences: Draw E1 and E2 mechanisms, Apply Zaitsev's Rule, Predict whether substitution or elimination dominates
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Organic Chemistry > Reaction Mechanisms
Subtopic: Electrophilic Addition to Alkenes
Content: Alkenes undergo electrophilic addition due to electron-rich C=C bond. With HBr: Markovnikov's rule (H adds to C with more H's). Mechanism: π electrons attack electrophile → carbocation → nucleophile attacks. With Br₂: forms cyclic bromonium ion intermediate. With H₂O (acid catalyst): hydration forms alcohol. With H₂ (Ni catalyst): hydrogenation forms alkane. Permanganate test: alkenes decolourise purple KMnO₄.
Competences: Draw electrophilic addition mechanisms, Apply Markovnikov's rule, Predict products of addition reactions
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Organic Chemistry > Functional Groups
Subtopic: Alcohols, Aldehydes, Ketones, and Carboxylic Acids
Content: Alcohols (R-OH): primary → aldehyde → carboxylic acid (K₂Cr₂O₇/H₂SO₄); secondary → ketone; tertiary → no oxidation. Dehydration: conc. H₂SO₄ at 170°C → alkene. Esterification: alcohol + carboxylic acid → ester + water (acid catalyst). Aldehydes (R-CHO): reduce Tollens' (silver mirror) and Fehling's (red ppt). Ketones (R-CO-R'): don't reduce Tollens'/Fehling's. Carboxylic acids: weak acids, react with bases/carbonates/metals. pKa values indicate acid strength.
Competences: Write reactions for oxidation of alcohols, Distinguish aldehydes from ketones using chemical tests, Write esterification equations
subject: chemistry
section: Alcohols, Aldehydes, Ketones, and Carboxylic Acids

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Organic Chemistry > Functional Groups
Subtopic: Amines, Amides, and Amino Acids
Content: Amines: primary (RNH₂), secondary (R₂NH), tertiary (R₃N). Basic properties: lone pair on N acts as proton acceptor. Basicity: aliphatic amines > ammonia > aromatic amines. Reactions: with acids → salts, with acyl chlorides → amides. Amides (RCONH₂): formed from carboxylic acids + amines; hydrolysed by acids/alkalis. Amino acids: contain both -NH₂ and -COOH; zwitterionic at isoelectric point; form peptide bonds (condensation polymerisation) → proteins.
Competences: Classify amines and compare basicity, Write reactions forming amides and peptide bonds, Explain zwitterion behaviour
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Physical Chemistry > Energetics
Subtopic: Enthalpy Changes and Hess's Law
Content: Standard enthalpy of formation ΔH°f: enthalpy change when 1 mole of compound forms from elements in standard states. Standard enthalpy of combustion ΔH°c: when 1 mole burns completely in O₂. Hess's Law: total enthalpy change is independent of route. ΔH°rxn = ΣΔH°f(products) - ΣΔH°f(reactants). Bond enthalpy: energy to break 1 mole of bonds in gas phase. ΔH ≈ Σ(bonds broken) - Σ(bonds formed). Born-Haber cycle: lattice enthalpy via Hess's Law for ionic compounds.
Competences: Calculate enthalpy changes using Hess's Law, Construct Born-Haber cycles, Use bond enthalpies to estimate ΔH
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Physical Chemistry > Chemical Kinetics
Subtopic: Rate Laws, Order, and Activation Energy
Content: Rate = k[A]^m[B]^n. Overall order = m + n. Determined experimentally (initial rates method or half-life method). Zero order: rate constant, [A] decreases linearly. First order: t½ = ln2/k (constant half-life). Second order: t½ = 1/(k[A]₀). Arrhenius equation: k = Ae^(-Ea/RT). ln(k) = ln(A) - Ea/(RT). Plot ln(k) vs 1/T gives straight line, gradient = -Ea/R. Catalysts lower Ea by providing alternative pathway.
Competences: Determine order from experimental data, Calculate rate constants and half-lives, Use Arrhenius equation to find activation energy
subject: chemistry
section: Rate Laws, Order, and Activation Energy

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Physical Chemistry > Chemical Equilibrium
Subtopic: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Constants
Content: Le Chatelier: system at equilibrium opposes change. Increasing [reactant] shifts right. Increasing T for exothermic shifts left (increases Kc); for endothermic shifts right (increases Kc). Pressure increase shifts toward fewer gas moles. Catalyst: no effect on position, only rate. Kc = [products]^n/[reactants]^m. Kp = (p_products)^n/(p_reactants)^m. Kp = Kc(RT)^Δn. Large K: products favoured. Small K: reactants favoured.
Competences: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle, Calculate Kc and Kp, Use ICE tables for equilibrium problems
subject: chemistry
section: Le Chatelier's Principle and Equilibrium Constants

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Physical Chemistry > Electrochemistry
Subtopic: Electrode Potentials and Electrolysis
Content: Standard electrode potential E°: measured against standard hydrogen electrode (SHE, 0.00 V). More positive E° = stronger oxidising agent. Cell EMF: E°cell = E°cathode - E°anode. Electrochemical series predicts feasibility. Nernst equation: E = E° - (RT/nF)ln(Q). Electrolysis: passage of electric current through ionic compound. Faraday's laws: m = MIt/(nF), where F = 96485 C/mol. Preferential discharge: ions with lower electrode potential discharged first at cathode.
Competences: Use electrode potentials to predict cell EMF, Apply Faraday's laws to electrolysis calculations, Predict products of electrolysis
subject: chemistry

text:
Subject: Chemistry
Topic: Physical Chemistry > Acids, Bases, and Buffers
Subtopic: pH, Buffers, and Titration Curves
Content: Brønsted-Lowry: acid = proton donor, base = proton acceptor. pH = -log₁₀[H⁺]. Strong acid: fully dissociates (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃). Weak acid: partially dissociates, Ka = [H⁺][A⁻]/[HA]. pH of weak acid: pH = ½(pKa - log[HA]). Buffer: resists pH change on addition of small amounts of acid/base. Acidic buffer: weak acid + conjugate base (e.g., CH₃COOH + CH₃COONa). Henderson-Hasselbalch: pH = pKa + log([A⁻]/[HA]). Titration curves: strong-strong (pH 7), weak acid-strong base (pH > 7 at equivalence).
Competences: Calculate pH of strong and weak acids, Prepare and explain buffer solutions, Interpret titration curves and select indicators
subject: chemistry
topic: Physical Chemistry > Acids, Bases, and Buffers

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Quadratic Functions
Subtopic: Solving Quadratic Equations
Content: Standard form: ax² + bx + c = 0. Methods: (1) Factorisation. (2) Completing the square: x² + bx = (x + b/2)² - (b/2)². (3) Quadratic formula: x = (-b ± √(b²-4ac))/(2a). Discriminant Δ = b²-4ac: Δ > 0 (two real roots), Δ = 0 (repeated root), Δ < 0 (no real roots). Sum of roots = -b/a. Product of roots = c/a. Forming equations: x² - (sum)x + (product) = 0.
Competences: Solve by factorisation, completing square, and formula, Use discriminant to determine nature of roots, Form equations given roots
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Polynomials
Subtopic: Factor and Remainder Theorems
Content: Factor theorem: if f(a) = 0, then (x - a) is a factor of f(x). Remainder theorem: when f(x) is divided by (x - a), remainder = f(a). Polynomial long division: divide to find quotient and remainder. Synthetic division for linear divisors. Solving cubics: find one root by trial (factor theorem), then factorise remaining quadratic. Partial fractions: proper fractions decomposed into simpler fractions. Linear factors: A/(x-a) + B/(x-b). Repeated: A/(x-a) + B/(x-a)². Irreducible quadratic: (Ax+B)/(x²+c).
Competences: Apply factor and remainder theorems, Perform polynomial division, Decompose into partial fractions
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Indices, Logarithms, and Exponentials
Subtopic: Laws of Indices and Logarithms
Content: Index laws: aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ, aᵐ/aⁿ = aᵐ⁻ⁿ, (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ, a⁰ = 1, a⁻ⁿ = 1/aⁿ, a^(1/n) = ⁿ√a. Logarithm: if aˣ = b then x = logₐb. Log laws: log(ab) = log(a) + log(b), log(a/b) = log(a) - log(b), log(aⁿ) = n·log(a). Change of base: logₐb = logcb/logca. Natural log: ln(x) = logₑ(x). Exponential function: y = eˣ, dy/dx = eˣ. Solving exponential equations: take log of both sides.
Competences: Simplify expressions using index laws, Solve equations using logarithms, Sketch exponential and logarithmic graphs
subject: mathematics
topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Indices, Logarithms, and Exponentials

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Sequences and Series
Subtopic: Arithmetic and Geometric Progressions
Content: AP: nth term aₙ = a + (n-1)d. Sum: Sₙ = n/2(2a + (n-1)d) = n/2(a + l). GP: nth term aₙ = arⁿ⁻¹. Sum: Sₙ = a(1-rⁿ)/(1-r) for r ≠ 1. Sum to infinity (|r| < 1): S∞ = a/(1-r). Sigma notation: Σ. Binomial expansion: (a+b)ⁿ = Σ C(n,r)aⁿ⁻ʳbʳ. C(n,r) = n!/(r!(n-r)!). Pascal's triangle gives coefficients. Valid for positive integer n. For fractional/negative n: (1+x)ⁿ = 1 + nx + n(n-1)x²/2! + ... (|x| < 1).
Competences: Find nth terms and sums of AP and GP, Determine convergence of geometric series, Apply binomial expansion
subject: mathematics
topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Sequences and Series

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Algebra > Inequalities
Subtopic: Linear and Quadratic Inequalities
Content: Linear: solve like equation but reverse sign when multiplying/dividing by negative. Quadratic: solve ax² + bx + c > 0 by finding roots and testing intervals (sign diagram). Critical values method: find where expression = 0, test regions. Modulus: |x - a| < b means a - b < x < a + b. |x - a| > b means x < a - b or x > a + b. Rational inequalities: find critical values (numerator = 0 and denominator = 0), test intervals.
Competences: Solve linear, quadratic, and modulus inequalities, Use sign diagrams, Represent solutions on number lines
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Calculus > Differentiation
Subtopic: Rules and Applications of Differentiation
Content: Power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹. Product rule: d/dx(uv) = u'v + uv'. Quotient rule: d/dx(u/v) = (u'v - uv')/v². Chain rule: d/dx(f(g(x))) = f'(g(x))·g'(x). d/dx(sin x) = cos x, d/dx(cos x) = -sin x, d/dx(tan x) = sec²x. d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ, d/dx(ln x) = 1/x. Implicit differentiation: differentiate both sides w.r.t. x, collect dy/dx terms. Applications: gradients, tangents (y - y₁ = m(x - x₁)), normals (gradient = -1/m), stationary points (dy/dx = 0, classify with d²y/dx²: positive = min, negative = max). Rates of change: connected rates via chain rule.
Competences: Apply all differentiation rules, Find tangents, normals, and stationary points, Solve connected rates of change problems
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Calculus > Integration
Subtopic: Methods and Applications of Integration
Content: Reverse of differentiation: ∫xⁿ dx = xⁿ⁺¹/(n+1) + C. ∫1/x dx = ln|x| + C. ∫eˣ dx = eˣ + C. ∫sin x dx = -cos x + C. ∫cos x dx = sin x + C. ∫sec²x dx = tan x + C. Substitution: let u = g(x), du = g'(x)dx. Integration by parts: ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du (LIATE for choosing u). Partial fractions: split rational functions before integrating. Definite integrals: ∫[a,b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a). Area under curve = ∫|f(x)|dx. Area between curves = ∫(f(x) - g(x))dx. Volume of revolution: V = π∫y²dx (about x-axis).
Competences: Integrate standard functions, Apply substitution and integration by parts, Calculate areas and volumes of revolution
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Calculus > Differential Equations
Subtopic: First-Order Differential Equations
Content: Separation of variables: dy/dx = f(x)g(y) → ∫(1/g(y))dy = ∫f(x)dx. First-order linear: dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x), integrating factor μ = e^(∫P dx), solution: y·μ = ∫Q·μ dx. Modelling: population growth (dN/dt = kN → N = N₀eᵏᵗ), radioactive decay (dN/dt = -λN → N = N₀e⁻λᵗ), Newton's cooling (dT/dt = -k(T - Tₛ)).
Competences: Solve by separation of variables, Use integrating factor method, Model real-world situations with differential equations
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Trigonometry and Coordinate Geometry > Trigonometry
Subtopic: Identities and Equations
Content: Pythagorean: sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ, 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ. Double angle: sin(2θ) = 2sinθcosθ, cos(2θ) = cos²θ - sin²θ = 2cos²θ - 1 = 1 - 2sin²θ. Addition: sin(A±B) = sinAcosB ± cosAsinB, cos(A±B) = cosAcosB ∓ sinAsinB. R-formula: a sinθ + b cosθ = R sin(θ + α) where R = √(a²+b²), tan α = b/a. Solving equations: find principal value, use symmetry/periodicity for all solutions in range. Inverse trig functions: arcsin, arccos, arctan domains and ranges.
Competences: Prove trigonometric identities, Solve equations in given intervals, Express a sinθ + b cosθ in R sin(θ + α) form
subject: mathematics
topic: Pure Mathematics — Trigonometry and Coordinate Geometry > Trigonometry

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Trigonometry and Coordinate Geometry > Coordinate Geometry
Subtopic: Lines, Circles, and Parametric Equations
Content: Straight line: y - y₁ = m(x - x₁). Gradient: m = (y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁). Perpendicular: m₁·m₂ = -1. Distance: d = √((x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)²). Midpoint: ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2). Circle: (x-a)² + (y-b)² = r², centre (a,b), radius r. Expanded: x² + y² + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, centre (-g,-f), radius √(g²+f²-c). Tangent to circle: perpendicular to radius at point of contact. Parametric equations: x = f(t), y = g(t). dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt). Convert to Cartesian by eliminating parameter.
Competences: Find equations of lines, circles, tangents, Convert between parametric and Cartesian forms, Find intersection points of lines and circles
subject: mathematics
topic: Pure Mathematics — Trigonometry and Coordinate Geometry > Coordinate Geometry

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Pure Mathematics — Trigonometry and Coordinate Geometry > Vectors
Subtopic: Vector Algebra and Applications
Content: Vectors: magnitude and direction. Position vector: r = xi + yj + zk. Magnitude: |r| = √(x²+y²+z²). Unit vector: r̂ = r/|r|. Addition: a + b (triangle/parallelogram law). Scalar product: a·b = |a||b|cosθ = a₁b₁ + a₂b₂ + a₃b₃. If a·b = 0, vectors are perpendicular. Vector equation of line: r = a + tb (a = point, b = direction). Scalar product form of plane: r·n = d. Angle between lines: cosθ = |a·b|/(|a||b|). Distance from point to line and plane formulae.
Competences: Perform vector addition and scalar product, Write vector equations of lines, Find angles between lines and planes
subject: mathematics
topic: Pure Mathematics — Trigonometry and Coordinate Geometry > Vectors

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Statistics and Probability > Data and Measures
Subtopic: Descriptive Statistics
Content: Measures of central tendency: mean (x̄ = Σx/n), median (middle value), mode (most frequent). For grouped data: mean = Σfx/Σf, median from cumulative frequency curve. Measures of dispersion: range, interquartile range (IQR = Q₃ - Q₁), variance (σ² = Σ(x-x̄)²/n), standard deviation (σ = √variance). For sample: s² = Σ(x-x̄)²/(n-1). Coding: if y = (x-a)/b then ȳ = (x̄-a)/b, σᵧ = σₓ/b. Box plots: show median, quartiles, range, outliers (beyond 1.5×IQR).
Competences: Calculate mean, median, mode for raw and grouped data, Calculate variance and standard deviation, Interpret box plots and cumulative frequency diagrams
subject: mathematics
topic: Statistics and Probability > Data and Measures

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Statistics and Probability > Probability Distributions
Subtopic: Binomial and Normal Distributions
Content: Binomial B(n,p): fixed n trials, two outcomes, constant p, independent. P(X=r) = C(n,r)p^r(1-p)^(n-r). Mean: μ = np. Variance: σ² = np(1-p). Normal N(μ,σ²): continuous, bell-shaped, symmetric about μ. 68% within 1σ, 95% within 2σ, 99.7% within 3σ. Standard normal Z ~ N(0,1): Z = (X-μ)/σ. Use tables to find P(Z < z). Normal approximation to binomial: if n is large and np > 5: X ~ N(np, np(1-p)) with continuity correction (±0.5).
Competences: Calculate binomial probabilities, Standardise and use normal distribution tables, Apply normal approximation to binomial
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Mathematics
Topic: Statistics and Probability > Hypothesis Testing
Subtopic: Significance Tests
Content: Null hypothesis H₀: no effect/difference (parameter = stated value). Alternative hypothesis H₁: one-tailed or two-tailed. Significance level α: typically 5% (0.05) or 1% (0.01). Test statistic: calculated from sample data. Critical region: values that lead to rejection of H₀. p-value: probability of observing result as extreme as sample, assuming H₀ true. If p-value < α, reject H₀. Type I error: rejecting H₀ when true (probability = α). Type II error: not rejecting H₀ when false. For proportions: Z = (p̂ - p₀)/√(p₀(1-p₀)/n). For means: Z = (x̄ - μ₀)/(σ/√n) or t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n).
Competences: Set up null and alternative hypotheses, Perform z-tests and t-tests, Interpret p-values and significance levels
subject: mathematics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Newton's Laws of Motion
Subtopic: Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia)
Content: A body remains at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external force. Inertia is the tendency of a body to resist changes in its state of motion. Mass is a measure of inertia.
Competences: Explain Newton's First Law with real-world examples, Distinguish between mass and weight, Describe inertia in everyday situations
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Newton's Laws of Motion
Subtopic: Newton's Second Law (F = ma)
Content: The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction of the force. F = ma, where F is the net force in Newtons, m is mass in kg, and a is acceleration in m/s². When mass is constant: F = ma. When mass varies (e.g., rockets): F = dp/dt. Impulse = F×t = change in momentum.
Competences: State and derive F = ma from momentum principles, Solve problems involving force, mass, and acceleration, Draw free body diagrams, Calculate impulse and momentum change
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Newton's Laws of Motion
Subtopic: Newton's Third Law (Action-Reaction)
Content: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. The action and reaction forces act on different bodies. They are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and of the same type. Examples: book on table (weight and normal reaction), walking (foot pushes ground, ground pushes foot), rocket propulsion (exhaust gases pushed down, rocket pushed up).
Competences: Identify action-reaction pairs, Explain why action-reaction pairs do not cancel out, Apply to walking, swimming, rocket propulsion
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Kinematics
Subtopic: Equations of Motion (SUVAT)
Content: For uniformly accelerated motion: v = u + at, s = ut + ½at², v² = u² + 2as, s = ½(u+v)t. Where u = initial velocity, v = final velocity, a = acceleration, s = displacement, t = time. These apply only when acceleration is constant. For free fall: a = g = 9.81 m/s² downward.
Competences: Apply SUVAT equations to solve motion problems, Interpret velocity-time and displacement-time graphs, Calculate displacement from area under v-t graph
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Kinematics
Subtopic: Projectile Motion
Content: Projectile motion is motion under gravity alone. Horizontal component of velocity is constant (no air resistance). Vertical component is affected by gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²). Time of flight: T = 2u sin(θ)/g. Range: R = u² sin(2θ)/g. Maximum height: H = u² sin²(θ)/(2g). Maximum range occurs at θ = 45°.
Competences: Resolve velocity into horizontal and vertical components, Calculate time of flight, range, maximum height, Sketch trajectories for different angles
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Circular Motion
Subtopic: Uniform Circular Motion
Content: An object moving in a circle at constant speed has centripetal acceleration directed toward the centre: a = v²/r = ω²r. Centripetal force: F = mv²/r = mω²r. Angular velocity: ω = 2πf = 2π/T. Period T = 2π/ω. Examples: car on a banked curve, conical pendulum, satellite in orbit. For a banked road with no friction: tan(θ) = v²/(rg).
Competences: Define centripetal acceleration and force, Solve circular motion problems with banking, Explain why centripetal force is not a separate force
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Work, Energy, and Power
Subtopic: Work-Energy Theorem and Conservation
Content: Work done: W = Fs cos(θ). Kinetic energy: KE = ½mv². Gravitational potential energy: PE = mgh. Elastic potential energy: PE = ½kx². Conservation of energy: total energy in an isolated system remains constant. Work-energy theorem: net work = change in KE. Power: P = W/t = Fv. Efficiency = (useful output / total input) × 100%.
Competences: Calculate work done by constant and variable forces, Apply conservation of energy to solve problems, Calculate power and efficiency
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Momentum and Collisions
Subtopic: Conservation of Momentum
Content: Linear momentum: p = mv. Conservation of momentum: total momentum before collision = total momentum after (in isolated system). Elastic collision: both momentum and KE conserved. Inelastic collision: momentum conserved, KE not conserved. Perfectly inelastic: objects stick together. Coefficient of restitution: e = relative speed of separation / relative speed of approach. e = 1 (elastic), 0 < e < 1 (inelastic), e = 0 (perfectly inelastic).
Competences: Apply conservation of momentum to collisions, Distinguish elastic from inelastic collisions, Calculate coefficient of restitution
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Mechanics > Gravitation
Subtopic: Newton's Law of Gravitation
Content: F = GMm/r², where G = 6.674 × 10⁻¹¹ N m² kg⁻². Gravitational field strength: g = GM/r². Gravitational potential: V = -GM/r. Orbital velocity: v = √(GM/r). Escape velocity: v = √(2GM/r). Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³, or T² = (4π²/GM)r³. Geostationary orbit: T = 24 hours, r ≈ 42,000 km from Earth's centre.
Competences: Apply Newton's law of gravitation, Calculate orbital velocity and escape velocity, Apply Kepler's Third Law
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Waves and Oscillations > Simple Harmonic Motion
Subtopic: SHM Equations and Energy
Content: SHM: acceleration proportional to displacement and directed toward equilibrium. a = -ω²x. Displacement: x = A sin(ωt + φ). Velocity: v = Aω cos(ωt + φ). Maximum velocity: v_max = Aω. Maximum acceleration: a_max = Aω². Period of mass-spring: T = 2π√(m/k). Period of simple pendulum: T = 2π√(l/g). Energy: E_total = ½kA² = ½mω²A². KE = ½mω²(A² - x²). PE = ½mω²x².
Competences: Derive and apply SHM equations, Calculate period of springs and pendulums, Analyse energy in SHM
subject: physics
topic: Waves and Oscillations > Simple Harmonic Motion

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Waves and Oscillations > Properties of Waves
Subtopic: Wave Parameters and Types
Content: Wavelength (λ): distance between successive crests. Frequency (f): oscillations per second (Hz). Period (T = 1/f). Amplitude (A): maximum displacement. Wave speed: v = fλ. Transverse waves: displacement perpendicular to propagation (light, water). Longitudinal waves: displacement parallel to propagation (sound). Intensity ∝ A². Inverse square law: I ∝ 1/r².
Competences: Define wave parameters, Apply v = fλ, Distinguish transverse from longitudinal waves
subject: physics
topic: Waves and Oscillations > Properties of Waves

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Waves and Oscillations > Properties of Waves
Subtopic: Interference and Diffraction
Content: Superposition: when waves overlap, resultant displacement = sum of individual displacements. Constructive interference: path difference = nλ. Destructive interference: path difference = (n + ½)λ. Young's double slit: fringe spacing Δy = λD/d. Single slit diffraction: minima at sin(θ) = nλ/a. Diffraction grating: d sin(θ) = nλ, where d = slit spacing.
Competences: Explain superposition principle, Solve Young's double slit problems, Apply diffraction grating equation
subject: physics
topic: Waves and Oscillations > Properties of Waves

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Waves and Oscillations > Properties of Waves
Subtopic: Stationary Waves
Content: Stationary waves form when two waves of same frequency travel in opposite directions. Nodes: points of zero displacement. Antinodes: points of maximum displacement. String fixed at both ends: L = nλ/2, f_n = nv/(2L). Open pipe: L = nλ/2. Closed pipe: L = (2n-1)λ/4 (odd harmonics only).
Competences: Explain formation of stationary waves, Calculate resonant frequencies of strings and pipes, Distinguish nodes from antinodes
subject: physics
topic: Waves and Oscillations > Properties of Waves

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Waves and Oscillations > Sound Waves
Subtopic: Speed of Sound and Doppler Effect
Content: Sound is a longitudinal wave. Speed of sound in air ≈ 340 m/s at 20°C. Speed increases with temperature: v = 331 + 0.6T (°C). Doppler effect: observed frequency changes when source or observer moves. f' = f(v ± v_o)/(v ∓ v_s). Approaching: frequency increases. Receding: frequency decreases. Beat frequency = |f₁ - f₂|.
Competences: Calculate speed of sound at different temperatures, Apply Doppler effect formula, Calculate beat frequency
subject: physics
section: Speed of Sound and Doppler Effect

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Waves and Oscillations > Optics
Subtopic: Reflection and Refraction
Content: Law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection. Snell's law: n₁ sin(θ₁) = n₂ sin(θ₂). Refractive index: n = c/v. Total internal reflection: sin(c) = n₂/n₁ (where c = critical angle). Applications: optical fibres, mirages, prisms. Thin lens formula: 1/f = 1/v - 1/u. Magnification: m = v/u. Power of lens: P = 1/f (in dioptres).
Competences: Apply Snell's law, Calculate critical angle for total internal reflection, Solve thin lens problems
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Electric Fields
Subtopic: Coulomb's Law and Electric Field Strength
Content: Coulomb's law: F = kQ₁Q₂/r², where k = 1/(4πε₀) = 8.99 × 10⁹ N m² C⁻². Electric field strength: E = F/q = kQ/r². Electric potential: V = kQ/r. Potential difference: V = W/q. Uniform field between parallel plates: E = V/d. Field lines point from positive to negative. Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to field lines.
Competences: Apply Coulomb's law, Calculate electric field strength and potential, Draw electric field patterns
subject: physics
section: Coulomb's Law and Electric Field Strength

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Direct Current Circuits
Subtopic: Ohm's Law and Resistance
Content: Ohm's Law: V = IR. Resistance: R = ρl/A (ρ = resistivity, l = length, A = cross-sectional area). Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂ + R₃. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂ + 1/R₃. Power: P = IV = I²R = V²/R. Kirchhoff's first law: ΣI = 0 at a junction. Kirchhoff's second law: ΣV = 0 around a loop. EMF = terminal PD + lost volts: ε = V + Ir.
Competences: Apply Ohm's Law and Kirchhoff's laws, Solve series and parallel circuits, Calculate power dissipation
subject: physics
topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Direct Current Circuits

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Direct Current Circuits
Subtopic: Capacitors
Content: Capacitance: C = Q/V. Parallel plate: C = ε₀εᵣA/d. Energy stored: E = ½CV² = ½QV = ½Q²/C. Capacitors in parallel: C_total = C₁ + C₂. In series: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂. Charging: Q = Q₀(1 - e^(-t/RC)). Discharging: Q = Q₀e^(-t/RC). Time constant: τ = RC.
Competences: Calculate capacitance of parallel plates, Solve series and parallel capacitor networks, Analyse charging and discharging curves
subject: physics
topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Direct Current Circuits

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Electromagnetic Induction
Subtopic: Faraday's and Lenz's Laws
Content: Magnetic flux: Φ = BA cos(θ). Faraday's law: EMF = -dΦ/dt = -N(dΦ/dt) for N turns. Lenz's law: induced current opposes the change that causes it. EMF in a rotating coil: ε = NBAω sin(ωt). Peak EMF: ε₀ = NBAω. Transformers: V₁/V₂ = N₁/N₂ = I₂/I₁ (ideal). Eddy currents: induced currents in bulk conductors, cause heating.
Competences: Apply Faraday's and Lenz's laws, Calculate induced EMF in rotating coils, Solve transformer problems
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Electricity and Magnetism > Alternating Current
Subtopic: AC Circuits and RMS Values
Content: AC voltage: V = V₀ sin(ωt). RMS values: V_rms = V₀/√2, I_rms = I₀/√2. Average power: P = V_rms × I_rms × cos(φ). Reactance: capacitive X_C = 1/(ωC), inductive X_L = ωL. Impedance: Z = √(R² + (X_L - X_C)²). Resonance when X_L = X_C: f₀ = 1/(2π√(LC)). Power factor: cos(φ) = R/Z.
Competences: Calculate RMS values, Solve AC circuit problems with impedance, Find resonant frequency
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Modern Physics > Quantum Physics
Subtopic: Photoelectric Effect
Content: Einstein's equation: E = hf = φ + ½mv²_max. Work function φ = hf₀ (threshold frequency). Planck's constant: h = 6.63 × 10⁻³⁴ J s. Stopping potential: eV_s = ½mv²_max = hf - φ. Photon energy: E = hf = hc/λ. Wave-particle duality: particles exhibit wave properties (de Broglie wavelength: λ = h/mv). Energy levels: E = -13.6/n² eV for hydrogen.
Competences: Apply Einstein's photoelectric equation, Calculate stopping potential and threshold frequency, Explain wave-particle duality
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Modern Physics > Nuclear Physics
Subtopic: Radioactivity and Nuclear Reactions
Content: Alpha (α): ⁴₂He nucleus, stopped by paper, highly ionising. Beta (β⁻): electron, stopped by aluminium. Gamma (γ): EM radiation, stopped by thick lead. Half-life: t½ = ln2/λ. N = N₀e^(-λt). Activity: A = λN = A₀e^(-λt). Mass-energy equivalence: E = mc². Binding energy per nucleon peaks at iron-56. Nuclear fission: heavy nucleus splits. Nuclear fusion: light nuclei combine. Both release energy.
Competences: Describe three types of radioactive decay, Calculate activity and half-life, Explain fission and fusion
subject: physics

text:
Subject: Physics
Topic: Modern Physics > Thermal Physics
Subtopic: Gas Laws and Thermodynamics
Content: Ideal gas law: pV = nRT = NkT. Boyle's law: pV = constant (constant T). Charles's law: V/T = constant (constant p). Pressure law: p/T = constant (constant V). Kinetic theory: pV = ⅓Nm<c²>. Average KE = 3kT/2. First law of thermodynamics: ΔU = Q - W. Specific heat capacity: Q = mcΔT. Latent heat: Q = mL.
Competences: Apply ideal gas law and gas laws, Derive pressure from kinetic theory, Apply first law of thermodynamics
subject: physics
